0: Behind the hospital, a low building. A tin door, the smell of phenol and melting ice. Outside stands a tempo, loaded at the back with a bolt of white cloth and pyre wood. Three or four men from the big house stand at the door. Bherulal's man is talking, cold-eyed, in no hurry. The mortuary compounder stands clutching his register to his chest.
1: Doctor Bhanwar closed the case yesterday itself, wrote it as death by a fall. Now hand over the body, the mother's waiting outside.
2: Sahib, a new order came from above this morning. The body's not to be released to anyone.
3: A new order? The file was closed yesterday.
4: It's come with Panwar sahib's signature, that the body goes to the district headquarters for a second postmortem. How can I release it on my own?
5: The man reads the register slowly, down to the name written below. Then he pulls a wad of notes from his vest and sets it on the open register.
6: Take this, and write that the family took the body. You've got the doctor's clean paper already.
7: The compounder doesn't earn this much in a year. But his hand stays right where it is.
8: Panwar sahib's coming himself, sahib. You'll get me trapped.
9: A woman's wailing rises from the corridor, and the other women hush her. The man looks at the cloth and wood in the tempo, then at the tin door.
10: Think it over. We need the body by evening. Let the mother do her daughter's last rites.
11: He walks back to his people. The compounder stands holding the register, the notes still on it. And Ratan has been standing at the door long enough to have heard it all. He looks at the tin door, then at the money on the register, and says nothing.
12: The Jhujhar song rises. Over it: that saka shadow from the cold open, the opium belt in first light, the CBN scales, a pen. Title: AMAL. Episode name: The Incision.
13: The world Ratan hasn't reached yet. Rana sits on his swing, a line of folded-hand petitioners before him, tea going round. He grants a licence to one, puts off the next, the same single smile for both. Apart from the line stands Kishan Borana, stiff-backed, white stubble beard, summoned, not come to grovel. He hasn't joined his hands. Rana has been working on him a while.
14: Kishan, sit down, man. How long have you been standing... All right. That land of yours by the Reva, the four-bigha plot, I'll pay you one and a half times the market rate, in cash, hand to hand. The road's coming through there, the whole area will benefit.
15: The land is my forefathers', sahib. Their blood and sweat is in it. It's not for sale. Forgive me.
16: Blood and sweat. Good. A man should be that attached to his land. And how's your boy? Karan? Doing pharmacy in Neemuch, isn't he.
17: For a moment something stops inside Kishan. Rana catches it and looks away.
18: I hear he's been keeping company with some boys who aren't right, the doda trade. If a case gets made tomorrow, the boy's whole life is ruined, the licence goes too. But don't you worry. The SP's my man, one word from me and Karan's name won't come up anywhere.
19: Kishan understands everything. His back is still stiff, but something behind his eyes has gone out.
21: Look at that! Kishan's a sensible man after all. Don't worry about the papers, I'll have them made, you just sign. And listen, Karan's remaining studies, I'll cover the cost now. He's my own boy.
22: He walks Kishan to the stairs like an honored guest. The old man goes down the steps, having just signed away his forefathers' land to save his son. Rana turns, claps for tea, and the line shuffles one step forward.
23: Ratan comes in. The compounder slackens with relief and points at the money.
24: Sahib, I didn't even touch it, he put it there himself.
25: Pick it up. It's not yours.
26: The compounder gathers the wad and puts it in the drawer. Ratan goes to the tin door but doesn't open it. On the other side is the file he signed in autumn, a name on the second page he never once turned back to look at. Sugna has come to stand at the inner door. Sitting among the women, she heard her brother's voice.
27: The case was cleared yesterday. We came to collect her today. You stopped it again.
29: She's been lying on ice four days, and now you'll take her two hundred kilometers away to be cut open again.
30: The report that's been made is a lie. I know how these are made, I've been making them twenty-five years.
31: You know so much. When she was being sold to old Dhanraj, a sixteen-year-old, where was all this knowing of yours then?
32: He has no answer. Sugna raised that girl and watched her be sold; Ratan only signed a paper.
34: Nobody killed her. She fell. That's what your department wrote. So let her last rites happen, brother.
36: She goes back into the dark. "Nobody" catches inside Ratan, a little too quick, a little too sure, but he lets it go as a mother's grief.
37: A small warm room, one tube light. Amma on the cot with her phone, a neighbor woman bent over it, both beaming at a photo. Manju sits to one side, eyes down, present and absent at once. Deva comes in late, the day still on him, he's spent the whole day with a dead sixteen-year-old girl, and now watches his sister's marriage being settled on a phone screen.
38: You're back! Look, a new photo of the boy came, standing with his tractor. They've got good land in Rajasthan. Manju will live there like a queen, a queen.
39: Deva looks at the photo a moment, then at his sister, who hasn't even lifted her eyes from her lap.
40: Is Manju looking at the photo too?
41: Oh, what will she look at, the poor thing's shy. It's a good family, Deva. Matches like this don't come every day.
42: Manju lifts her eyes to her brother for a moment, then lowers them back to her lap. Deva says nothing. The girl from the mortuary is still behind his eyes, and his own sister sits three feet away while her life is being settled on a phone.
43: All morning, the fan turning, two farmers waiting with their files and folded notes. The munshi is quietly on the phone, and puts it down fast the moment Ratan walks in.
45: Nothing, sahib. Mishra sahib was asking whether you'd come in.
46: Ratan sits. The first farmer slides his file and a fold of notes across the desk. Ratan signs the file and slides the notes back with one finger. The farmer stares at him; this has never happened before.
49: The farmer takes his money back and leaves, a little offended. The munshi watches and says nothing. Deva, at the next desk, is watching too.
50: Sahib, the body business is all over the bazaar now. Bherulal seth's men have been circling the hospital since morning. And Mishra sahib has called three times.
52: He pulls a file toward himself. Deva steps closer and lowers his voice.
53: Sahib, what you wrote last night, that two-hundred-kilometer thing, will the body really reach there? Won't those people stop it?
54: They'll stop it. Watch how they stop it.
55: Mishra stands at the window. He turns with that same usual warmth, but this morning he's already made three calls.
56: Sit, brother. Yesterday it seemed the matter was settled. Today I hear you slapped down a second order, district headquarters. Ratan, a report's already in, plain and clear, death by a fall. The paper's complete, the file's closed. Now what's the point of a second postmortem, brother?
57: Because the first one came the same day.
58: So? The doctor was quick about it.
59: You and I have had that quickness done for twenty-five years, Mishra. A proper postmortem takes a week. A report that came the same day, we both know what it means.
60: The warmth thins. He tries reason, it's the one weapon he has.
61: All right, say you're right, the girl didn't fall. So what? This system's been running twenty-five years, Ratan, before you and I ever came. Now, at retirement, you'll set it straight? That girl won't come back from ten postmortems. Who are you doing all this for, brother?
62: Ratan doesn't answer. He can't, not in any way Mishra would understand. Mishra waits, and the silence doesn't move the way his silences usually do. The warmth drains from his face, and what's under it is just tiredness.
63: All right, brother. I've explained, I've done my duty. Now this won't stay in my hands, it'll go up, to Rana sahib. Then don't say Mishra didn't warn you.
64: Ratan stands. He's heard that name his whole life; it's the one that got his father's licence cancelled. He walks out without a word.
65: The house Ratan grew up beside and never once went inside. Big, cold, full of money, somewhere a color TV blaring, a deep freezer humming, the papers for a new tractor on the table. Bherulal sits at a low table counting notes, one wad, a rubber band, then another wad, like a man counting grain. His cold-eyed man waits.
66: Did you pay the hospital fellow?
67: I did, seth. The munshi, the compounder, all settled. Just that Panwar...
68: I'll handle Panwar. He's our own man, family, he'll understand.
69: Sugna has come to a stop at the inner door, a steel glass going cold in her hands. She's heard it all and she hasn't moved. Bherulal feels her there but doesn't look up.
70: Go inside. This is men's work.
71: What price did my daughter go for? She had a price on her alive, and a price on her dead.
72: I'm not the one pricing her, Sugna. The world prices her. I'm saving the house. I have a four-year-old son, the licence has to be made in his name. Should I sink the whole house for one girl?
73: Sugna looks at him, then at the money, and goes back into the dark of the house without a word. Bherulal looks at the empty doorway. For a moment his hands rest on the notes; then he ties the next wad tighter than before and doesn't look toward that door again.
74: And if someone asks about the girl?
75: She fell. Slipped off the field bank, hit her head. It's been four days now, even God will write the same thing.
76: A white SUV stands before the gate. Bherulal stands by it, no longer in mourning now, a big man solving a problem, the cold-eyed man a step behind. The moment Ratan comes out, he throws his arms open.
77: Arre, brother-in-law sahib! There's mourning at home and you're here buried in papers. Come, come home, have some tea. Your sister's crying.
78: How did the girl fall, Bherulal?
79: The same thing again. Look, brother-in-law sahib, I'm a businessman, I'll talk straight. What do you want, land? Money? Want me to place the boy somewhere? Whatever you ask will be in front of you by this evening. Just drop this body business right here. It's only a matter of paper.
81: Truth! Houses don't run on truth, sahib. I have a four-year-old son, next year the licence has to be made in his name. For that the house's name must stay clean. And you, his own maternal uncle, are putting a stain on that name, for a girl who isn't even here anymore.
83: Daughters there are more of. A son, there's only one.
84: He turns toward the SUV. Then stops, as if some small thing has come to mind, and nods, not to Ratan, but to his cold-eyed man.
85: It's a long road, two hundred kilometers, Panwar sahib. Empty and deserted early in the morning. A man alone, an old car, anything can happen.
86: Last month a sahib went down this same road at night. The bike slipped, finished on the spot. It's a bad road.
87: He says it without any heat. By then Bherulal is already in the SUV, warm again, hand raised in farewell.
88: Do think it over. The tea will be ready.
89: The SUV pulls away. Ratan will take that same road tomorrow.
90: Ratan and Deva in the hospital office. The clerk behind the counter is very sorry but doesn't budge an inch.
91: A vehicle? There's only one hearse, sahib, and it's broken down today, at the mechanic's.
92: Will it be fixed by tomorrow?
93: Can't say, the parts come from Indore. Come back after a week. The body will stay here on ice till then, what's the hurry.
94: There's a hurry. The family wants to do the last rites and—
96: A government body in a private vehicle? There's no rule for it, sahib. You need a form, approval from above. And in cases like this, approval isn't coming through these days. You understand, don't you.
97: Ratan understands. The same hand is on every door, two hundred kilometers up, never having to show itself. He doesn't waste his anger on a small man behind a counter.
99: Outside, Deva burns with it, the way only the young burn.
100: Sahib, they've stopped the vehicle on purpose. We complain higher up—
101: Higher up. Higher up is the very one who got the vehicle stopped, Deva. Paper won't work anymore. We'll have to find another way.
102: By the riverside the pyre stands built and ready, wood stacked, ghee set out, the priest's fee paid and he sits idle, and no body for the pyre. The family sits around it in the dust, the women in a circle, Sugna in the middle, dry-eyed while the rest weep. Bherulal's cold-eyed man moves among the men standing at the edge, dropping a word here, a word there, and the whole town has chosen its side. Ratan comes down the path; the road passes right by this field and he has to come through here. The talk stops ahead of him. An old woman sees him and says it to his face.
103: Here he comes. This is him. Her own maternal uncle, and he won't let the mother do her daughter's last rites, four days now. Is this police rule or bandit rule?
104: A low murmur runs through the standing men. Ratan stops. He looks at the empty pyre, at the wood that won't be burned, and then past it at Sugna. For a moment his sister turns and looks at him. She isn't pleading, isn't weeping. Then she turns her face away. Ratan walks on through that silence, and the men make way for him.
105: Tempos and trucks parked for the night, the lone bulb of a tea shop. Ratan, out of uniform, stands with a wary tempo driver.
106: Tomorrow morning, four o'clock, district headquarters, one trip. It's government work, but the vehicle's not government. The responsibility is mine.
109: The man's hand half-closes on the money.
110: Bherulal seth's one? Forgive me, sahib, I can't make an enemy of that house. My business is right here. Take it today and tomorrow my vehicles are grounded.
111: He hands the money back. Ratan takes it without arguing. He knows how big a thing he's asking. Just then Deva speaks from the dark; he'd come looking and heard the last part.
112: Sahib, my uncle has a tempo, across the border on the Rajasthan side. He's an outsider, his business isn't here, beyond Bherulal's reach. I'll talk to him, and I'll come along too.
113: Ratan looks at the boy a moment, then nods once.
115: The two of them stand together a moment in the dark.
116: A different world entirely, set apart from the belt: a high gate, a paved road, and a lawn kept green in a district where the wells have run dry. Cars, some men carrying guns who walk like police but aren't. Ratan and Deva in the borrowed jeep. Deva takes the sweep of it all in.
117: All this belongs to one MLA?
118: MLA's a recent thing. First his father was a bandit in the ravines. Then came land, then votes. Now he hands out licences, gives to whom he likes, takes from whom he likes.
119: A man at the gate waves them in without checking; they're expected. Ratan has gone completely still.
120: A wide veranda. Rana holds court at the center of it on a swing, in white, a big carefree man, a phone in one hand, a line of petitioners before him. He settles each in a single breath, granting to one, putting off another, never in a hurry. The moment he sees Ratan he rises, arms open, like an old friend has arrived.
121: Arre, Panwar sahib! Look who's here. Come, come, sit. This is your own house, sahib, your own house.
122: Ratan doesn't sit. He stands a little too long, taking in that green lawn, the cars, the gunmen who walk like police but aren't, and Rana goes on watching him take it all in.
123: My house was beyond Garoth, Rana sahib. It went with the licence.
124: For half a moment the room goes quiet. Rana's smile doesn't fall; it deepens, as if Ratan has said something lovely.
125: Do sit, now. Look at this one, that Chambal tale, the man who went alone into the ravines among the bandits, that's him. Your sahib was a hero in his day, son.
126: I haven't come to tell tales, Rana sahib. I've come to talk about the Koteshwar girl.
127: Straight to the point. There'll be time for that too, sahib. First tea, first a little warmth. You gave the government a whole life. Now's the time to receive. A man should get, in the end, what's his due. Isn't that so?
128: A servant brings the kasumba things, opium, water, and the cupped palm. Rana takes it before the servant can serve and mixes the kasumba with his own hand, that old hospitality, refusing which is taken as an insult.
129: No, no, this I'll give with my own hand, the old way. People these days have forgotten all this, I don't forget. It's this same earth that gives us everything, sahib, medicine and honor both, it all comes out of this one flower.
130: Ratan looks at the offered palm and doesn't take it at once. They both know what this is: a thing taken from this hand has to be returned to this hand.
131: Pour it with your hand, and one day you'll come asking too, Rana sahib.
132: For the first time Rana's pleasure is real, not put on. He likes that the old man sees it.
133: See! That's why you're a hero. But what a guest gives is not returned, sahib. It's the custom.
134: A moment. Then Ratan drinks from his hand, because in this house, from this palm, a man does not refuse, and he takes it the way a man finally signs a paper he's read all the way through. Rana watches him, certain he's just tied a thread around the old man's wrist.
135: The petitioners have been cleared away. Now just the two of them, and Deva at the edge. Rana comes to the matter with no change in his warmth.
136: Let me ask you something, don't take it ill. Your father's licence, the one beyond Garoth, what year was it cancelled?
137: In seventy-eight. And it was cancelled on your father's weighing. Ask that too, Rana sahib.
138: Silence. Deva is stunned. But Rana only nods slowly, sorrowing over it, as if Ratan has reminded him of something.
139: It came up two kilos short, yes. Quarrels of the fathers, sahib. You got ground in it, and to tell the truth, so did we. But that's an old matter. I can set it right. One phone call, and that licence comes back in your family's name. That seal ring too, the one lying pawned at the seth's, let it be redeemed. A man pawning his forefathers' keepsake, it doesn't look right.
140: That seal ring. He even knows about the ring. Ratan's face doesn't move, but on the wall he came in leaning against, a hand now rests.
142: Rana looks struck by the word, as if Ratan has mistaken a kindness.
143: Price? There's no price, sahib. I'm not getting you to do anything. That Koteshwar girl, she fell, poor thing, that's what the paper says too. Just let her stay fallen. Give the body to the family, let them do the last rites. I'm not telling you anything new. What you've done for twenty-five years, just look the other way one more time.
144: Rana is asking nothing of him, just to stay the same man he's been for twenty-five years, and to turn his face away one more time. This goes deeper than any threat. Ratan's face doesn't move, but on the wall he came in leaning against, both hands now rest.
145: The dead girl won't come back, sahib. But a living family, your own, its licence can come back.
146: This is the very thing Ratan has wanted for twenty-five years, set warmly before him, in the same cup he's already drunk from. His father's licence. The ring back on his finger. And all of it for one signature, which he's made a thousand times. He's turned his face away for far less. He looks at Rana's open, friendly face, and at that green lawn grown on his family's dead water, and stays silent a long time.
148: Then all the better. You're doing it for your own blood. For family.
151: He neither argues nor explains. He sets down the empty cup and turns to leave. For a moment the warmth vanishes from Rana's face. The host steps back and the thing seated beneath him peers out and weighs Ratan.
152: Think it over, Panwar sahib. Nobody's in a hurry. But don't take too long either. Two years is a long time, and a very short one too.
153: Ratan walks out through the waiting petitioners. Behind him Rana is warm again, calling the next man.
154: The jeep on the road, the green gate shrinking behind. Deva is driving. He's just watched a man be offered everything, say one word, and walk out, and he can't hold it in.
155: Sahib, that licence was your family's. And the ring too. You refused.
157: For a girl who isn't even here anymore.
158: You're saying it too. Mishra says it, Bherulal says it, Rana says it. They all say the same thing. Why, for a dead girl. Nobody asks who she was for when she was alive.
159: Deva has no answer. He drives. He'd moved his whole family to a border town so his sister could marry into a good house, and today the two halves of his day have come face to face inside him.
160: On the deserted highway in the dark the tempo runs, Deva's uncle's, with an out-of-state plate. The cloth-wrapped body is in the back. Ratan in front, Deva in the middle, the driver hunched at the wheel. A Bolero has been behind them a long while, headlights steady, keeping its distance.
161: Sahib, that Bolero. It's been on us since the station.
163: Deva, what a job you've landed me in, brother. A body, and a vehicle behind us. I'm an outsider here.
164: Keep driving. Don't stop, no matter what happens.
165: Ahead, where the road narrows over a culvert, a tractor-trolley stands across it. Two men stand by it, not putting much effort into moving it. Behind them the Bolero is closing in.
166: The road's blocked. Sahib, I was telling you—
167: Don't look back. Take it down into the field, run along the bank.
168: The driver hesitates; the tempo isn't built for fields. The tractor men start walking toward them. Deva is half out of his seat.
170: The driver takes the tempo down off the road onto the shallow bank, jolting along the edge of the field, the body bouncing in the back, and climbs back onto the road past the culvert. The Bolero gets stuck behind the very tractor put there to trap them. The men shout. The tempo pulls away into the dusk.
171: They came to stop us. All this for one body.
172: Nobody does all this for one body. The man hiding it is afraid of something else.
173: Mishra on the phone, the warmth gone, all business, calling ahead before the body.
174: Yes, district headquarters hospital, forensics. Put me through to Doctor Khare. ...Doctor sahib, this is Mishra, Amargarh CBN. A body's coming to you, the Koteshwar one. Look, the case is already closed, the report's clean. This second postmortem is just one cranky officer's whim, the poor man's at retirement. You understand, no need to dig too much.
175: He listens. Whatever's said from the other end is short, and not what he wanted. The smoothness falters.
176: ...No, that's fine, Doctor sahib. Forgive me, sorry to trouble you.
177: He puts the phone down and sits looking at it. Some doors are so high up the road that his hand can't reach them.
178: A big, hard hospital at the city's edge. The tempo comes in; Ratan and Deva carry the wrapped body inside and no one helps. They meet Doctor Khare with a clipboard, fifties, rumpled coat, tired eyes.
179: The Koteshwar one? Ratan Singh Panwar. You sent her? A call just came from Amargarh, saying don't dig too much.
181: I've got six bodies today. No Amargarh seth has done me any favors. Whatever I see, I'll write. I don't have time for nonsense.
182: For the first time the road has reached a place the hand from above doesn't.
183: A cold room, a steel table, a drain in the floor. Khare opens the girl with the quick, unhesitating hands of someone who's done this ten thousand times. Ratan stands at the head. Deva, by the wall, has lost his color. Khare studies him a moment before beginning.
184: Female, age sixteen, seventeen. Injury to the back of the head. ...Hmm.
187: He picks up the blade. The moment the first incision is made Deva turns away, then walks out with a hand over his mouth. Ratan doesn't look away. He's signed a hundred such papers without once standing in the room. This one he stands for.
188: Later, the work done. Khare wipes his hands and tells them flatly, the way he'd tell of any one of the day's other six bodies.
189: This girl didn't die from this head injury.
191: Meaning when this injury was made, she was already dead. There's no blood pooled under the wound, and in a living body it pools. After death her head was struck against the bank, later. There are pressure marks on the throat, fine specks of blood in the eyes. She was strangled. She didn't fall, she was killed first and then made to look fallen.
193: What Ratan had known since he saw that low field bank was finally said in a clean room, by a man with no stake in it.
195: That's your job. I can only tell you that someone killed her up close, with their own hands. And no girl lets a stranger that close.
196: Ratan says nothing. He's thinking of a big house full of family.
197: Outside, the day is too bright after that cold room. Deva sits on a step, wrung out. Ratan comes out with the folded report in hand, two typed pages and a stamp. Khare follows, lighting a cigarette.
198: Inspector, a piece of free advice. This paper's yours now. The Amargarh people didn't want it made, which means there's a big man's name in it, or a name it leads to. I'm two hundred kilometers away, nothing will happen to me. But you live right among them. Be careful, sahib.
199: It's far too late to be careful. Twenty-five years.
200: He doesn't explain it and Khare doesn't ask. Deva looks up at him from the step. They've won nothing; they've only made one thing true, and now it has to be carried.
201: On the way back, in a town that isn't theirs, Ratan stops at a small post office. He photographs both pages of the report on Deva's phone. Then he buys an envelope, writes his own home address on it, seals the real report inside and posts it. Across the counter comes a registry receipt and he tucks it away carefully.
202: Sahib, you've got the report already. Why post it?
203: What I have can be taken, can be burned. But what's been logged in the post, with a government stamp and date, no one can erase now, whatever happens to me.
204: Deva hears that last bit and doesn't like it. Ratan walks back toward the tempo, and the report sets off behind him through the post, toward home.
205: Much later that night. Deva's come home from the district headquarters, the incision still in his eyes, he stood in a cold room and watched what was done to a sixteen-year-old girl who'd said no. Amma's on the phone in the next room, happy; the Rajasthan match is all but settled. Manju sits with her books she no longer opens. Deva sits down across from her and asks the thing no one in this house asks.
206: Manju. This Rajasthan match. What do you yourself want? Tell me the truth.
207: Maa's seen it, hasn't she. Whatever you all think is right.
208: Leave Maa. I'm asking you. Do you want to go into that house?
209: Manju opens her mouth and finds she has no answer ready. No one ever taught her to want anything of her own, let alone to say it. She looks toward her mother's voice in the next room.
211: It's the truest thing said in this house in weeks. Deva looks at his sister, and the girl on the steel table won't leave the room. He doesn't press her; he has nothing to give her yet.
212: Manju has gone in. Under Amma's brightness there's a worry now; after the photos went round, the boy's side called.
213: They said one thing. For the wedding the boy wants a Bullet, a motorcycle, and a little gold. It's a big house, their standing has to be kept up too.
214: A Bullet and gold. How much?
215: Deva, all this just happens. If your father were here he'd also—
216: This is dowry, Maa. In plain words, dowry.
217: Not dowry, it's custom. It's give and take. It happens in every house. If a girl's to be married off, you have to give. Why are you being like this today?
218: Deva doesn't answer. He spent four days in another district learning a girl's market price, and now that same price has followed him home, sat down at his own table, and is calling itself custom.
219: On foot, with no papers, Ratan finds the village's human wreckage: Ganga, a doda-husk addict, dry and trembling, propped against the wall behind the country liquor shop. A man with nothing left to lose, and that's what makes him the one man who'll talk.
220: You lie out here at night, near the fields?
221: Where would I go, sahib. The doda's in every vein. I belong nowhere now.
222: That night, the night of Bherulal's girl, did you see anything?
223: I did. Late at night there was light in the field, torches burning. Two or three men, Bherulal seth's, the big-car ones. Carrying something toward the field bank.
225: Something heavy wrapped in cloth. I turned my face away, sahib. Whoever keeps his eyes open at night here doesn't get to see the morning. And I'm half dead anyway.
226: Late night. Ratan sits at his table with the copy of the report, working out the knot of it, half to Deva and half to himself.
227: The match with Dhanraj was settled. Bherulal's weighing had been short two years running, the licence was shaky. The girl's sale would clear the debt and raise the match too.
229: The whole village heard her shouting, that she wouldn't go to that old man. That night she died. His men laid her on the field bank to make it look like a fall. And in the morning everyone was bent on cremating her in a hurry.
231: I'm saying every road circles back to that one house.
232: Deva has gone. Ratan sits alone with the picture he's built. The motive, the men, the cover-up, the haste, all point to that big house. One piece doesn't sit straight with the rest. Khare's words: killed up close, by hand, by someone she let near. Bherulal's men can carry a body to the bank in their big cars, but no girl lets a hired man put hands on her throat in the dark. Whoever did it, she knew, and trusted. Ratan turns the thought over once. It takes him somewhere he isn't ready to go. He sets it aside, tells himself the men are enough, and writes at the top of the page: Bherulal. Below that name the page is blank.
233: Ratan is out. The munshi slides a file toward Deva, a fold of notes tucked in the cover, the daily machinery runs on, inspector or no inspector.
234: Deva, just sign this. It's an accident, a straight case. And take this tea-money, it's your share.
235: Deva looks at the file, then at the money. A week ago this would have made no difference to him. Now there's a girl on a steel table behind his eyes, and his sahib walking empty-handed out of a kingdom.
236: The file's done. You keep this.
237: Caught Panwar sahib's sickness, son? It'll wear off in two weeks. It's a job, it doesn't run like this.
238: Deva says nothing. He's done nothing brave. He signed off the accident just like everyone does, only he didn't take the money, and even that small thing feels lonely.
239: Govind finds Ratan on the steps by the water and sits down beside him without hurry, the way an old friend does.
240: I hear you've made an enemy of the whole department. Even Rana. For a dead girl.
241: Have you come to say the same thing too?
242: No. I've come to say it's good to see you like this after years. Twenty-five years you walked around dead, Ratan. A corpse in uniform. Now something's stirring inside you. You've picked a bad time for it, two years left, the pension's on the line. But the topsoil's coming off. Underneath is the same old man.
243: Maybe it's coming off too late.
244: Late, then. We all have to die, Ratan. Some people just wake up once before they die.
245: On the way back. Deva has carried this whole day, the dowry sitting at his own table, and the money he didn't take, and now it comes out.
246: Sahib, today I didn't take the money from the munshi. But I know, in two weeks I'll start taking it. Everyone takes it, you took it too. So what's the difference? One girl, one report, what'll it change? You're ruining your whole life and nothing will change.
247: Ratan walks a while before answering, and what he gives the boy is no hero's answer, because he doesn't have one.
248: Nothing will change. And there's no gain in it either.
249: Then why are you doing it?
250: Twenty-five years I signed every body, went home, ate, and slept. This time the sleep didn't come. That's all it is.
252: He says nothing more. At the turning where their paths split, he stops, and without looking at the boy, says.
253: The day your sleep goes too, don't run from it. That's the one thing that'll stay alive in you.
254: He walks off. Deva stands at that turning a long time.
255: Bherulal has come to him, and this time the seth isn't calm, the report reached the district, the body was cut open, and Panwar is still going. Rana listens from his swing, feeding bits of fruit to a caged bird.
256: Rana sahib, this is slipping out of hand. The report's been made, it's reached the district. That man isn't stopping. You said you'd handle it.
258: Till when, sahib? He's my son, the licence has to be made in his name next year...
259: I offered that man his father's cancelled licence back, Bherulal. Mixed the kasumba with my own hand for him. And he said no to my face.
260: He watches the bird hop in its cage.
261: Very few people say no to me. And the ones who do, I remember them.
262: He picks up the phone, and the warmth that returns is just a habit his face performs.
263: Yes, listen. That Koteshwar matter, Panwar's. The poor man's tired, he's come to retirement. All this running about isn't good for the health. Get him a little rest. At home.
264: He puts the phone down and turns back to the bird. He never says the word suspension; for that there are other people.
265: Mishra brings the order himself, which in its own way is a kindness. He isn't enjoying it. He's genuinely sorry, and that's the harder thing to bear.
266: You've been suspended, the inquiry's pending. I told you, Ratan, it went all the way up. Hand in your badge and revolver. The pension stays frozen till the inquiry's done, and the inquiry's never done, you know that too.
267: Ratan unpins the badge and sets it on the desk. Twenty-five years. He doesn't argue. There's no one to argue with, only Mishra, who is himself just a man the machine uses.
269: It's done. Now let it go, yaar. The badge is gone, the pension's going. Now for whom?
270: Ratan doesn't answer. For the first time in twenty-five years he turns and walks out without a badge. Mishra sits looking at what's left on the desk.
271: Ratan at home, without a badge. Kanta has heard; the town hears everything. She serves the food, but today there's an edge in it that silent years had kept down. The pension papers she'd left in a drawer lie out on the table today.
272: The office women came today. Asking if it's true, that you've been suspended, the pension frozen. I told them I don't know. My husband never tells me anything anyway.
274: Two years. Just two years were left. You thought the same, didn't you, a little peace after two years. And you blew it all in one stroke. For some dead girl you don't even know.
276: She said the thing she never says. The kitchen goes completely silent. Ratan looks at the papers, and at his wife, who stopped expecting anything of him years ago and today waits for an answer. He can't give her the true answer, that the girl was his sister's child, that he'd sold her on paper without ever looking. He gives her what he has.
277: I know her. And I don't.
278: It's no answer and she knows it. But beneath it she hears something that hasn't been in his voice for twenty-five years, the man she married, who was different before the dust settled. This frightens her more than the lost pension. This time she doesn't put the papers away. She sits down across from him, which she hasn't done in years, and nudges the plate a little closer.
279: Eat your food. It's getting cold.
280: Ratan on a bench in a big officer's outer room, a file on his knee, an old man in plain clothes, no badge. A PA passes him twice. The third time he stops, clearly told what to say.
281: Sahib's in a meeting. And anyway you're suspended right now, officially you can't bring any case. Send your complaint by post, it'll be looked at.
284: Ratan looks at that closed door, behind which a man he served under for years won't meet him. He spent his life inside doors like these, watching them close on others. He gets up, tucks the file under his arm, and leaves.
285: No badge, no path, outside every book. Ratan stands across the dark field from the big house glowing at its far edge. His sister's house, the house his theory points to. Deva comes to stand beside him, out of uniform too.
286: Sahib, you're suspended. I'll get trapped along with you.
287: Go home, Deva. This is my fight.
288: You said it yourself, the day your sleep goes, don't run from it. Mine's gone, sahib.
289: Ratan doesn't send him back again. The two of them stand looking across the dark at that glowing house.
290: Bherulal. From tomorrow we start with that house.
291: Inside those glowing windows his sister sits, and her brother stands out here in the dark, deciding to begin with the wrong man.
292: A man walking home through the sleeping belt in the dark, no badge, no pension, no path, a file under his arm and a registry receipt in his pocket. By every measure of this place he's lost. He's blown those two years, the pension, his place, his wife's peace, all of it. By every rule of the principles he lived by, this is the place where a man stops.
294: The Jhujhar song rises, the voice of the Charan, of the headless one who fights on even after falling.
295: Last image: an old man alone on a dark road, walking toward that glowing house.
